structural: Bento grid supplies rigid modular containment; glassmorphism adds depth-perception layering on top of that rigid grid. The two systems solve complementary problems — grid solves WHERE, glass solves WHAT-COMES-FORWARD. Dark mode is the required substrate (glass needs contrast to read as glass).
tradeoff: Glass surfaces tank WCAG text contrast if not carefully tuned. Cap glass blur at 12-16px and lock text to ≥4.5:1 against the blurred backdrop, not against the raw backdrop.
contrarian: Score held back from 10 by either narrow audience or one real failure mode.

structural: Brutalism strips all decoration — bare borders, raw type, no shadows. Neon accents become the ONLY color signal, so the eye is forced to them. Kinetic motion adds urgency. The combination works because brutalism creates a low-signal canvas that amplifies the neon.
tradeoff: Fatigues users after 30+ seconds. Use only on landing/hero — switch to a calmer system for app surfaces.
contrarian: A contrarian would say: drop two of the four elements — the combo is doing too much.

structural: Liquid gradient provides organic color movement; ambient motion keeps it alive without user input. Pure atmosphere combo — high emotion, low information. Works only when the brand promise IS the feeling (meditation, music, luxury).
tradeoff: Zero information density. Pair with a hard-information section immediately below or users leave with no memory of what you do.
contrarian: A contrarian would say: drop two of the four elements — the combo is doing too much.

structural: Editorial typography (large display serif + small body sans) signals authority; bento grid modernizes the otherwise-newspaper feel; progressive disclosure lets readers scan then dive. Strong combo because each element addresses a different reader-mode (scan, skim, deep-read).
tradeoff: Requires real editorial discipline — bad typography choices will read as 'trying too hard'. Hire an editor or stick to a system like Newsreader + Inter.
contrarian: Score held back from 10 by either narrow audience or one real failure mode.

structural: The textbook high-synergy combo. Skeletons reduce perceived latency during load; micro-interactions provide feedback once loaded. Together they cover the full wait→response cycle. Every SaaS app should ship this by default.
tradeoff: None meaningful. The only failure mode is over-animating micro-interactions to the point of distraction — cap at 200ms.
contrarian: Score held back from 10 by either narrow audience or one real failure mode.

structural: Stacks four motion systems at once. High ceiling, low floor — looks incredible when executed by a senior motion designer, falls apart in junior hands because the four systems fight for attention. Score is conditional on execution.
tradeoff: Bundle size (GSAP + Three.js + Lottie easily 250kb+), accessibility (must respect prefers-reduced-motion), and SEO (pure JS sites don't index). Use sparingly.
contrarian: A contrarian would say: skip this combo entirely. The aesthetic is the message, not the system.

structural: All four elements share a single visual gene (high-contrast light-on-dark). High coherence but low versatility — locks you into one aesthetic. Works for crypto/gaming because the audience signal-boosts the aesthetic; fails for B2B SaaS.
tradeoff: Chromatic aberration and scanlines both reduce legibility. Disable for body text, restrict to display only.
contrarian: A contrarian would say: drop two of the four elements — the combo is doing too much.

structural: Minimal layout + glass surfaces + film grain = 'expensive magazine' feel. Noise grain is the secret ingredient — it adds organic imperfection that pure-digital glass lacks. Duotone imagery keeps the palette controlled.
tradeoff: Noise grain at high opacity murders image compression (PNGs balloon). Use SVG noise overlay at 3-5% opacity, capped.
contrarian: A contrarian would say: drop two of the four elements — the combo is doing too much.

structural: Claymorphism (soft shadows + rounded + pastel) + tilt-on-hover + cursor-reactive = tactile toy feel. All elements serve the same emotional goal (friendly), so coherence is high. Score capped at 7 because the audience is narrow.
tradeoff: Excludes enterprise/finance/healthcare audiences entirely. Don't ship this to a banking client.
contrarian: A contrarian would say: drop two of the four elements — the combo is doing too much.

structural: Four elements all signaling 'network motion'. Coherent but expensive to execute (Three.js particle systems) and rarely justifies the cost. Score held back by ROI — most telecom sites would convert equally well with a static hero.
tradeoff: Three.js particle fields cost 80-150kb and a senior dev. Use only when the product itself is a network/edge product (Cloudflare, Vercel, Fastly).
contrarian: A contrarian would say: skip this combo entirely. The aesthetic is the message, not the system.

structural: Vignette + depth-blur = lens compression; kinetic typography = title sequence; ambient motion = background score. Each maps to a film-language element. Strong combo for media brands because the metaphor is exact.
tradeoff: Vignettes and blur reduce information density. Use on hero only, fade to clean layout on scroll.
contrarian: A contrarian would say: drop two of the four elements — the combo is doing too much.

structural: Isotype (pictogram quantities) replaces bar charts for non-technical readers; bento organizes the report; skeleton handles data load; duotone controls palette. Each element solves a distinct problem in the data-reporting pipeline. Underrated combo.
tradeoff: Isotype scaling breaks down past 100 units (you can't draw 1,000 icons). Hybridize: isotype for ≤100, switch to bar chart above.
contrarian: Score held back from 10 by either narrow audience or one real failure mode.

structural: GSAP ScrollTrigger is the engine; stagger gives rhythm; spring physics gives weight; magnetic cursor gives intimacy. All four are motion primitives that compose cleanly. The combo is the modern agency portfolio standard for a reason.
tradeoff: Magnetic cursors break touch devices. Detect coarse-pointer and disable. ScrollTrigger pin sections break if user has smooth-scroll extensions — test in production.
contrarian: Score held back from 10 by either narrow audience or one real failure mode.

structural: AI products have a unique UX problem: long async operations with uncertain completion. Ambient motion signals 'the model is thinking'; progressive disclosure surfaces results incrementally; mesh gradient signals 'this is a new kind of product'. Each element addresses a real AI-UX failure mode.
tradeoff: Mesh gradients can dominate visual hierarchy. Keep mesh at 30-40% opacity behind text, never as the primary surface.
contrarian: Score held back from 10 by either narrow audience or one real failure mode.

els: skeleton loading, KPI cards, sparklines, filter
structural: The platonic SaaS dashboard. Skeleton handles load; KPI cards deliver the headline answer; sparklines provide trend at a glance; filter chips let users self-serve. Every element earns its place. This is what every analytics product should ship.
tradeoff: Sparklines without axis labels mislead. Always pair with min/max or a tooltip — bare sparklines invite pattern-matching bias.
contrarian: Score held back from 10 by either narrow audience or one real failure mode.

structural: Editorial layout + dark-native (not 'dark mode', but designed dark-first) + vignette for focus + kinetic typography for drama. Premium media brands (NYT Magazine, The Atlantic dark mode) use this combo. The vignette is what separates 'dark mode' from 'dark-native'.
tradeoff: Dark-native means designing for dark FIRST, then adapting to light — most teams do the reverse. Requires designer buy-in from day one.
contrarian: A contrarian would say: drop two of the four elements — the combo is doing too much.

structural: Claymorphism softens the cold edge of pure minimalism; floating labels reduce visual noise without losing affordance; soft shadows add depth without harshness. The combo is dominant in healthtech (Calm, Headspace, Hims) because it signals safety.
tradeoff: Floating labels fail screen-reader announces if not implemented with aria-label. Use the Radix or shadcn pattern, not a hand-roll.
contrarian: Score held back from 10 by either narrow audience or one real failure mode.

els: bento grid, tabular nums, sparklines, duotone
structural: Tabular nums align numerical columns; sparklines compress trends; bento organizes panels; duotone prevents palette overload. Built for power-user surfaces (Bloomberg terminal, Linear, Vercel). Score held below 10 because it intimidates non-expert users.
tradeoff: Don't ship this as your landing page. Use it for the in-app product surface; pair with a friendlier marketing site.
contrarian: Score held back from 10 by either narrow audience or one real failure mode.

structural: High self-expression, narrow audience. Variable fonts give kinetic type without GSAP; tilt 3D gives parallax depth; chromatic aberration signals 'digital native'. All three share a 'break the rules' energy. Score capped because the combo actively repels 35+ audiences.
tradeoff: Chromatic aberration triggers migraines in some users. Add a toggle and respect prefers-reduced-motion.
contrarian: A contrarian would say: skip this combo entirely. The aesthetic is the message, not the system.

structural: The dev-tool aesthetic is now a recognized language. Monospace type signals 'this is for engineers'; dark-native is the expected substrate; syntax highlighting is functional, not decorative. Zero wasted elements. Every dev tool from Linear to Vercel to Raycast uses some variant.
tradeoff: Overused — your dev tool will look like every other dev tool. Differentiate via ONE unexpected element (e.g. a single warm accent color, like Linear's purple).
contrarian: Score held back from 10 by either narrow audience or one real failure mode.
